front: Work: Lady Philosophy wears a garment with pi at the bottom and theta at the top, connected by a staircase, in this work written in prison
Work: Book V argues that the distinction between divine intellect and human reason allows divine prescience to coexist with free will
Work: compares God's timeless perspective to a point at the center of a circle, equidistant from its entire circumference
Work: compares God to a ship's 'rudder and helm' and echoes the Gorgias in claiming that evildoers punish themselves by no longer truly existing as humans
Work: Joel Relihan argued this work is structured as a Menippean satire to advocate the necessity of prayer and faith
Work: a poem in this work addressed to the 'lord and path and goal' is traditionally interpreted as a summary of the Timaeus
Philosopher: his translations of Aristotle were virtually the only Aristotelian works available to medieval Europeans until the 12th century
Philosopher: his translation of Porphyry's Isagoge became the standard medieval logic textbook and codified the 'problem of universals'
Philosopher: argued that written words signify spoken words, which signify thoughts, which denote things, a theory he termed the 'order of speaking'

Philosopher: his De institutione musica divided music into musica mundana (celestial harmony), musica humana (bodily harmony), and instrumental music, the most influential medieval source for Greek musical thought
Philosopher: his De Topicis Differentiis defines topics as 'parts of an argument that give confidence' and juxtaposes treatments by Cicero and Themistius
